body.p:
When you want to process XML instances from different sources with XSLT, you will inevitably have to deal with the topic of namespacesXML ConstructsNamespaces to avoid conflicts in the element selectors.
UWE test footnote (9/40, callout I): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.

Especially with chained transformations, it can also happen that a namespace is unexpectedly generated in the output that the following processing step does not understand because it was not declared there.

There are several ways to declare a namespace in the stylesheet. Assuming that exactly one source and at most one configuration file is processed in a transformation step, the stylesheet element could look like this:

li.p: The xslnamespace is of course the namespace for the XSLT instructions and must be declared.

The xsnamespace is necessary if you want to work with typed values. It allows the inclusion of data types according to the XML Schema specification XML Schema and thus better validation of the stylesheet at compile time.
The declaration of a custom prefixed namespace allows the inclusion of custom XSLT functions, such as the inclusion of the FunctX library FunctX
The unprefixed namespace is the default namespace and can handle a namespace from the input
The xpath-default-namespace attribute specifies another namespace that can be used in XPath functions. In this field I would specify the namespace of a configuration or separate data file.

body.p: In my view, more than one namespace in the input should be avoided in XML processing with XSLT - if possible. If necessary, it is advisable to normalize the input before processing and rename elements if needed. Otherwise, you can also declare custom namespace prefixes, such as:
and use them in XPath selections and match rules.

messagepanel.typeofhazard: If the input data contains namespaces that are not handled in the XSLT stylesheets - the namespace may only be attached to one very specific element - it can lead to unexpected results during the transformation - without an error message.
messagepanel.howtoavoid: Therefore, the data should be analyzed very carefully regarding namespaces beforehand.

body.p: Namespaces in the input are therefore usually handled via the header declaration in the stylesheet file. Which namespace is ultimately written to the output depends on the currently processed context node:

li.p: Elements that you create automatically receive the default namespace (unless you explicitly specify a namespace).
Elements that you copy carry the namespace they had in the input (unless you explicitly prevent this).

body.p: There are several ways to control (or override) these two default behaviors:

Here, an element is declared with the unprefixed namespace of the context node. If the context node does not have a different namespace, this ensures that the default namespace actually appears in the output.
Here, an element with a custom namespace specification is written to the output. More simply written:
There is also an attribute on the xsl:copy element that can control the namespace copying process:
Here, the namespace on the p element is not written to the output.
Likewise, a default copy rule can be used that prohibits inheriting a namespace:

section.title: In the Wild
section.p: In the wild, I recently stumbled upon the following problem:

messagepanel.typeofhazard: Declared this way, the new element [[code:my-element]] would be placed in the output with an empty namespace, like [[code:<neuer-name xmlns=""/>]]. A subsequent transformation step cannot read this.
messagepanel.howtoavoid: For this reason, I always place new elements in the output using the element constructor.
